SLOPE 1.2.0.9999

    • Changelog

Models in SLOPE

Johan Larsson

2026-01-06

Source: vignettes/models.Rmd
models.Rmd

Models

Sorted L-One Penalized Estimation (SLOPE) is the procedure of minimizing objectives of the type minimize{F(β0,β;X,y)+J(β;α,λ)}, \mathrm{minimize}\left\{F(\beta_0, \beta; X, y) + J(\beta; \alpha,\lambda) \right\}, where J(β;α,λ)=α∑j=1pλj|β|(j), J(\beta; \alpha,\lambda) = \alpha \sum_{j=1}^p \lambda_j \lvert \beta \rvert_{(j)}, with α∈ℝ+\alpha \in \mathbb{R}_{+}, λ∈ℝ+p\lambda \in \mathbb{R}_+^p and (j)(j) represents an rank of the magnitudes of β\beta in descending order. λ\lambda controls the shape of the penalty sequence, which needs to be non-increasing, and α\alpha controls the scale of that sequence.

XX and YY are the design matrix and response matrix, respectively, which are of dimensions n×pn \times p and n×mn \times m respectively. Except for multinomial logistic regression, m=1m = 1.

We assume that FF takes the following form: F(β0,β)=1n∑i=1nf(β0+xi⊺β,yi), F(\beta_0, \beta) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n f(\beta_0 + x_i^\intercal \beta, y_i), where ff is a smooth and convex loss function from the family of generalized linear models (GLMs), xix_i is the iith row of the design matrix XX, and yiy_i is the iith row of the response matrix YY.

SLOPE currently supports four different models from the family of generalized linear models (GLMs):

  • Least-squares (Gaussian) regression,
  • Logistic regression,
  • Multinomial logistic regression, and
  • Poisson regression.

Due to the way GLMs are formulated, each model is defined by three components: a loss function f(η,y)f(\eta, y), a link function g(μ)g(\mu), and an inverse link function g−1(η)g^{-1}(\eta). Here, η\eta is the linear predictor, and μ\mu is the mean of the response variable.

Loss functions, link functions, and inverse link functions for generalized linear models in the SLOPE package. Note that in the case of multinomial logistic regression, the input is vector-valued, and we allow log\log and exp\exp to be overloaded to apply element-wise in these cases.
Model f(η,y)f(\eta, y) g(μ)g(\mu) g−1(η)g^{-1}(\eta)
Gaussian 12(y−η)2\frac{1}{2}(y - \eta)^2 μ\mu η\eta
Binomial log(1+eη)−ηy\log(1 + e^\eta) - \eta y log(μ1−μ)\log \left(\frac{\mu}{1 - \mu}\right) eη1+eη\frac{e^\eta}{1 + e^\eta}
Poisson eη−ηye^\eta - \eta y log(μ)\log(\mu) eηe^\eta
Multinomial ∑k=1m−1(log(1+∑j=1m−1eηj)−ykηk)\sum_{k=1}^{m-1}\left( \log \left( 1 + \sum_{j=1}^{m-1} e^{\eta_j}\right) - y_k \eta_k \right) log(μ1−μ)\log\left(\frac{\mu}{1 - \mu}\right) exp(η)1+∑j=1m−1eηj\frac{\exp(\eta)}{1 + \sum_{j=1}^{m-1} e^{\eta_j}}

Gaussian Regression

Gaussian regression, also known as least-squares regression, is used for continuous response variables, and takes the following form: f(β,β0;X,y)=12n∑i=1n(yi−xi⊺β−β0)2. f(\beta, \beta_0; X, y) = \frac{1}{2n} \sum_{i=1}^n \left(y_i - x_i^\intercal \beta - \beta_0\right)^2.

You select it by setting family = "gaussian" in the SLOPE() function. In the following example, we fit a Gaussian SLOPE model to the bodyfat data set, which is included in the package:

library(SLOPE)

fit_gaussian <- SLOPE(
  x = bodyfat$x,
  y = bodyfat$y,
  family = "gaussian"
)

We can plot the solution path:

plot(fit_gaussian)

We can also print a summary of the fitted model:

summary(fit_gaussian)
#> 
#> Call:
#> SLOPE(x = bodyfat$x, y = bodyfat$y, family = "gaussian") 
#> 
#> Family: gaussian 
#> Observations: 252 
#> Predictors: 13 
#> Intercept: Yes 
#> 
#> Regularization path:
#>   Length: 82 steps
#>   Alpha range: 0.00136 to 2.55 
#>   Deviance ratio range: 0 to 0.749 
#>   Null deviance: 69.8 
#> 
#> Path summary (first and last 5 steps):
#>    alpha deviance_ratio n_nonzero
#>  2.55000          0.000         0
#>  2.32000          0.112         1
#>  2.12000          0.206         1
#>  1.93000          0.283         1
#>  1.76000          0.347         1
#>  . . .
#>   0.00197          0.749        13
#>   0.00180          0.749        13
#>   0.00164          0.749        13
#>   0.00149          0.749        13
#>   0.00136          0.749        13

SLOPE also contains standard methods for returning coefficients and making predictions:

coef(fit_gaussian)
#> 14 x 82 sparse Matrix of class "dgCMatrix"
#>                                                                               
#>  [1,] 19.15079 13.95994658 9.2302401 4.9207076 0.9940222 -2.5838274 -5.8438306
#>  [2,]  .        .          .         .         .          .          .        
#>  [3,]  .        .          .         .         .          .          .        
#>  [4,]  .        .          .         .         .          .          .        
#>  [5,]  .        .          .         .         .          .          .        
#>  [6,]  .        .          .         .         .          .          .        
#>  [7,]  .        0.05608334 0.1071844 0.1537458 0.1961708  0.2348268  0.2700488
#>  [8,]  .        .          .         .         .          .          .        
#>  [9,]  .        .          .         .         .          .          .        
#> [10,]  .        .          .         .         .          .          .        
#> [11,]  .        .          .         .         .          .          .        
#> [12,]  .        .          .         .         .          .          .        
#> [13,]  .        .          .         .         .          .          .        
#> [14,]  .        .          .         .         .          .          .        
#>                                                                             
#>  [1,] -8.8142241 -11.5207361 -13.9868090 -16.2338028 -18.2811798 -20.1466735
#>  [2,]  .           .           .           .           .           .        
#>  [3,]  .           .           .           .           .           .        
#>  [4,]  .           .           .           .           .           .        
#>  [5,]  .           .           .           .           .           .        
#>  [6,]  .           .           .           .           .           .        
#>  [7,]  0.3021418   0.3313837   0.3580278   0.3823049   0.4044254   0.4245807
#>  [8,]  .           .           .           .           .           .        
#>  [9,]  .           .           .           .           .           .        
#> [10,]  .           .           .           .           .           .

#> [13,]  .           .           .           .           .           .

#>                                                                            
#>  [1,] -1.727754e+01 -17.355195651 -17.414414095 -17.486024660 -17.547966237
#>  [2,]  6.272088e-02   0.062669703   0.062609555   0.062565133   0.062521430
#>  [3,] -8.282978e-02  -0.083314417  -0.083729994  -0.084154855  -0.084535781
#>  [4,] -7.428004e-02  -0.073878622  -0.073580064  -0.073221304  -0.072897235

#>  [7,]  9.386209e-01   0.940024017   0.941336101   0.942518416   0.943608978

#> [11,]  1.581548e-01   0.159544977   0.160663598   0.161846336   0.162935751
#> [12,]  1.708524e-01   0.171782183   0.172656490   0.173442284   0.174165123
#> [13,]  4.429153e-01   0.443743667   0.444453680   0.445122122   0.445736635
#> [14,] -1.611375e+00  -1.612254116  -1.612918235  -1.613516156  -1.614202520
#>                                                                            
#>  [1,] -17.602600657 -17.653369502 -17.703692909 -17.746166463 -17.785107997
#>  [2,]   0.062479782   0.062446446   0.062411501   0.062383868   0.062357005
#>  [3,]  -0.084875829  -0.085188506  -0.085487036  -0.085747766  -0.085985579
#>  [4,]  -0.072611775  -0.072342380  -0.072093366  -0.071868390  -0.071671836
#>  [5,]  -0.468609675  -0.468789604  -0.468953212  -0.469112201  -0.469215847
#>  [6,]  -0.019875391  -0.020235385  -0.020553206  -0.020848008  -0.021115868
#>  [7,]   0.944608594   0.945509572   0.946339395   0.947079700   0.947762810
#>  [8,]  -0.200379071  -0.201020275  -0.201591758  -0.202104136  -0.202602908
#>  [9,]   0.229165339   0.229788173   0.230348774   0.230844448   0.231322891
#> [10,]   0.005911397   0.006729014   0.007505445   0.008200801   0.008834764
#> [11,]   0.163898634   0.164823359   0.165628150   0.166364522   0.167052025
#> [12,]   0.174820810   0.175411724   0.175968607   0.176492562   0.176921803
#> [13,]   0.446286069   0.446802874   0.447276866   0.447689569   0.448074220
#> [14,]  -1.614760003  -1.615346233  -1.615731910  -1.616226456  -1.616620907
#>                    
#>  [1,] -17.820381266
#>  [2,]   0.062331745
#>  [3,]  -0.086202479
#>  [4,]  -0.071482533
#>  [5,]  -0.469374051
#>  [6,]  -0.021355919
#>  [7,]   0.948386482
#>  [8,]  -0.203044217
#>  [9,]   0.231752150
#> [10,]   0.009386911
#> [11,]   0.167669450
#> [12,]   0.177342343
#> [13,]   0.448422133
#> [14,]  -1.616955686

y_hat <- predict(fit_gaussian, x = bodyfat$x[1:5, ], alpha = 0.01)

plot(bodyfat$y[1:5], y_hat)

Logistic Regression

Logistic regression is used for binary classification problems, and takes the following form: f(β0,β;X,y)=1n∑i=1n{log(1+exp(xiTβ+β0))−yixiTβ}, f(\beta_0, \beta; X, y) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \left\{ \log\left(1 + \exp\left(x_i^T \beta + \beta_0\right)\right) - y_i x_i^T \beta \right\}, where XiX_i is the iith row of the design matrix XX.

You select it by setting family = "binomial" in the SLOPE() function.

References

On this page

Developed by Johan Larsson, Jonas Wallin, Malgorzata Bogdan, Ewout van den Berg, Chiara Sabatti, Emmanuel Candes, Evan Patterson, Weijie Su, Jakub Kała, Krystyna Grzesiak, Mathurin Massias, Quentin Klopfenstein, Michal Burdukiewicz.
